data.title: The Real Brokerage: Why I Believe It's A Hidden Gem In Real Estate Tech
data.description:
The Real Brokerage Inc (NASDAQ:REAX) is a real estate company founded in 2014 and is present in almost all of the United States and 4 Canadian provinces. It is not just a real estate company; I consider it one of the most technologically advanced compared to the companies I analyzed previously, and more agents are joining the company.

The technological edge

REAX hopes to achieve its agents with the greatest possible freedom of action by brokering through its proprietary software, "reZEN". Its advantage over the competition lies in its technology, as some traditional franchises lack proprietary software and outsource this function.

REAX's latest innovations have included an AI-based virtual assistant called Leo CoPilot, launched in the fourth quarter of 2024, and a debit card for agents in the US and a credit card for agents in Canada called Real Wallet, already used by 3,200 agents with an average account balance of $8 million.

This technological efficiency of REAX is further reflected in LTM Adjusted Operating Expenses (% of Revenue), which increased from 7.2% in 2022 to 5.1% in 2024. This is also reflected in Agents Per Full-Time Brokerage Employee, which increased from 98 in 2022 to 136 in 2024.

I consider this initial data more than sufficient for you to understand the technological advantage of REAX, which does not need physical offices to operate, as it is enough to have efficient software and thus has an insignificant initial cost for each new agent.

I believe this technological potential is clearly driving the growth of its agents, which increased from 8,200 in 2022 to 24,140 in 2024, an increase of 194%, and I consider this represents, and in a sector as difficult as the current United States real estate sector, a clear demonstration that REAX is a great buy opportunity.

Investment thesis

I want to begin my investment thesis by looking at REAX's agent growth in recent years compared to other companies in the sector that also gained agents. That is, in the chart below, I only included companies that gained agents, not those that lost them, and the result is that REAX is the one that grew the most.

For this example, I used the following companies: Compass (COMP), Newmark Group (NMWK), Redfin (RDFN), and Douglas Elliman (DOUG). The companies I didn't include because they are losing agents are Anywhere Real Estate (HOUS), RE/MAX (RMAX), and eXp World Holdings (EXPI). Others, such as Marcus & Millichap (MMI) or Opendor Technologies (OPEN), don't provide precise numbers on the number of agents. I think the chart is clear; REAX not only had the largest increase, but it also surpassed Compass, one of its most capable competitors, in agents.

Regarding the results for Q1'25, the latest report available, REAX recorded 26,870 agents (a 61% year-over-year increase), although, at the time of the Q1'25 presentation in May, they already had 27,700, which implies, in my opinion, that Q2'25 will continue to show an upward trend.

Another important point to mention is the contribution of agents to gross revenue, which was $353 million in Q1'25, up 76% compared to $200 million in the same period last year. This growth is in line with the trend of increasing agents and with the Cost of Sale, which increased 77%, in line with revenue (these are the commissions paid by REAX to its agents).

However, I want to note that Gross Profit rose from $20 million to $33 million, a strong gain that led the company to reduce its net losses and be on the verge of achieving operating profitability. Furthermore, its operating expenses grew 7% YoY, while its Gross Profit increased 63%, so at this rate, profitability is only a matter of time. I believe that these good results are largely due to the technological appeal I explained earlier, which adds agents without the need for office or administrative expenses.

In addition to all these data, the company's share price, in my view, does not reflect this growth in revenue, agents, or the modern technological tools that REAX provides to its agents, as I explained above.

If we look at Seeking Alpha's financials section, REAX's P/S is 0.55x, compared to the industry average of 4.29x. What's more, compared to its competitors, this ratio is also significantly lower (only surpassed by HOUS, although the latter's debt burden is due to its large volume of debt, and OPEN, which has an SA warning for poor performance), demonstrating a negative market assessment, though I can't find an explanation for this pessimism. It's possible, at least at first glance, that investors are wary of the bearish momentum facing the US real estate sector. But REAX's rise goes beyond the current economic situation.

Private Listing

Now, I want to highlight a topic that is proving quite controversial in the United States real estate sector, and it concerns the National Association of Realtors (NAR) requirement that every home listed for sale be publicly listed within one business day of its publication and must be added to the Multiple Listing Service (MLS).

REAX hasn't taken a position yet (unlike Compass, which has initiated a legal dispute with the NAR over its, in my opinion, rather rigid stance in favor of private listings), but REAX, for its part, conducted an internal survey of its agents, where 65% responded that private listings won't make a big difference in terms of revenue from home sales.

The company asked several internal questions about this, but I want to stick with the question of whether using private listings is better for agents: the response was that more than half (54%) disagree.

Based on the agents' responses, their position is primarily that they disagree with the exclusive listings, or at least that they are neutral, meaning they would agree to follow NAR policies, which, in my opinion, would save the company from legal disputes. For its part, no REAX executive has commented on the matter so far.

Q1 2025 Results

Now, I want to analyze the Q1'25 results and focus on REAX's challenge with operating profitability, as the net result was a loss of $5 million, a loss that, I believe, is explained by the process the company is undertaking in its bid to acquire more and more agents.

What is more, one only needs to look at the reduction in losses in recent years to realize the company's growth; falling from $27 million to $25 million from 2023 to 2024, and if I annualize the loss for this Q1'25 (of $5 million), a conservative estimate gives me a net loss of around $20 million for all of 2025. In fact, in the quarterly variation, the company reduced its net losses by 18%.

I've already explained how operating expenses are growing far below gross profit, but it's worth noting that one of the costs detailed by the company relates to research and development, a section I highlighted in yellow and which, in my opinion, reflects REAX's commitment to continuing to invest in technology.

Another highlight, (which in my opinion is very positive for REAX's balance sheet given the difficult context of the real estate sector) is that it has no debt, a cash position of $32 million available at the end of 2024, and will reach $54 million in Q1'25 if we also include non-cash. The company is open to taking on debt whenever it deems necessary to invest in new technologies, but for now, the numbers add up perfectly.

In the meantime, it is financing itself with share buybacks, as it did in 2024 with a $34 million share repurchase, and as the company's Board of Directors recently announced it would do in 2025, seeking a $150 million share repurchase. As of March 31, 2025, the company has over 204 million shares, a 10% YoY increase. As losses narrow, as I've shown they are happening, confidence in REAX is likely to continue growing, so I think it's time to buy.

Valuation

To objectively validate my thesis that REAX is undervalued, I performed a valuation using multiples, developing two models: one relatively conservative by incorporating negative RDFN ratios that further penalize the final estimate, and another more optimistic one by neutralizing the negative ratios and leaving them at 0.0 without penalizing the final result. In both scenarios, REAX is undervalued.

I used the following multiples: P/S, BV/Share, FCF/Share, and P/BV, all shown in the Seeking Alpha pairs section. I believe that all multiples reflect the reality of the sector in terms of poor or non-existent operating profitability, and even, in the case of RDFN (which has SA alert and its shareholders approved its sale to Rocket Companies), it reflects its crisis with a P/BV of -8.39x and a BV/Share of -1.23x. I gave a slightly higher weighting, of 35%, to FCF/Share, since I consider it the most suitable to measure the real value of the stock in relation to its capacity to generate cash compared to the other ratios.

I allowed myself to use these ratios, although negative, in the conservative valuation model, and REAX still gives me an upside of 81%, with a target price of $7.27. Removing these negative ratios and leaving them at 0.0 so as not to distort the final result gives me an upside of 128%, but the conservative model is enough for me to understand that REAX is undervalued.

Risks

I believe REAX has few risks. But if I had to mention one, I would say that a tense real estate sector environment could slow sales and reduce revenue, an impact that could come from various factors, from high mortgage rates to the FED's interest rates remaining at their current high levels for a prolonged period, to a general decline in real estate transactions.

In fact, REAX's own sales index indicates that sales fell to 47.8 in April from 50.2 in March, and this is something that affects all companies operating in the real estate sector. At this point, just as strong agent growth gives REAX a winning edge over its peers, a sharp drop in sales could make it difficult to achieve operating profitability.

It's also worth considering among the legal risks, not so much the debate over private listings, but rather internal litigation, related to employee complaints or other types of allegations that could hinder REAX and represent higher expenses, as occurred in Q1'24, when the company spent $9 million, although so far, this has not been repeated.

Conclusion

I see REAX as a technological leader in the real estate sector. Its strong agent growth demonstrates its operational capacity for continued growth, and although it still has net losses, the confidence it generates in the stock market and the company's share buybacks allow it to finance itself in the medium term without resorting to debt. I believe the market is not fully appreciating this potential, and that's why I see REAX as an excellent buy opportunity.

data.title: United Homes Group: Further Upside Is Warranted
data.description:
For years now, I have been a big fan of the housing industry. Perpetual undersupply, a generally stable asset, and the ability to benefit from a growing population are all things that I love to see in an investment opportunity. So you can imagine my elation when I was able to dig into United Homes Group (NASDAQ:UHG) earlier this year. Unlike many of the other homebuilders that I have analyzed in recent years, this particular play is quite small. As of this writing, it has a market capitalization of $211.6 million. Like many of the other players in the industry, it has found itself under pressure because of worsening market conditions. High home prices have reduced demand for housing in the near term. Furthermore, high interest rates aimed at combating inflationary pressures, and now aimed at ensuring that the US economy does not experience more inflation because of the trade war situation that is going on, have ultimately served to push demand lower as well.

Unfortunately, United Homes Group has experienced weakness because of these issues. Even so, when I last wrote about the company in March of this year, I found myself impressed by the core markets that the company operates in, the fact that it has a strategy of optioning lots instead of owning them outright in order to keep costs low in the near term, and I also found myself impressed by some improvements such as those involving net new orders and the pricing of homes closed. These factors caused me to rate the company a ‘buy’ even though finance performance had been admittedly mixed. Since then, investors have been well rewarded, with the stock shooting up 16.9% at a time as the S&P 500 is up 9.1%. Even though I think the easy money has been made because of this, I do believe that it still justifies, if only marginally, maintaining a ‘buy’ rating.

A look at recent results

If you're not familiar with United Homes Group, the company operates as a designer, builder, and seller, of homes in South Carolina, North Carolina, and Georgia. Because I covered this in more detail in my previous article, I don't think it would make sense to dig into the specifics of those markets again. But it is important to keep a couple of things in mind. For starters, in these high-growth markets, the company enters into options contracts in order to buy lots instead of doling out the capital to buy them upfront. In fact, around 99% of the 7,500 lots that the company has fall under third-party lot option contracts or land bank option contracts. On top of this, this is not just any homebuilder. While it does have some diversification, including duplex houses, townhouses, and attached single-family houses, most of its business is dedicated to the construction and sale of detached single-family homes. These cater to not only entry-level buyers, but also first, second, and third-move-up buyers.

Despite these operational advantages that the firm has over some other players in the space, recent financial performance has been disappointing. In my previous article, I was able to cover the final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. Now, results extend through the first quarter of 2025. During that time, revenue was $87 million. That represents a decline of 13.7% compared to the $100.8 million the business reported one year earlier. According to management, this drop was in spite of the fact that the average price of a home closed improved nicely from $335,057 to $344,784. And it was driven by a decline in the number of homes closed from 311 to 252.

Management described market conditions in the home building industry as being negatively affected by elevated mortgage rates, macroeconomic conditions, and heightened geopolitical uncertainty. In the image below, you can see the gap that exists between median household income and the amount of income somebody would need in order to buy an average home in the US without spending more than 30% of their annual income on housing costs. Inflationary pressures and high interest rates can be thanked for most of this worsening in recent years. And on the inflationary side, that seems to be largely the result of continued effects from what were temporary supply chain shortages during and after the COVID-19 pandemic. Management also blamed, in the first quarter earnings call, ‘unusually poor weather’ in South Carolina.

It shouldn't be surprising to see a drop in revenue beget a drop in profits. Net income fell from $24.9 million last year to $18.2 million this year. Other profitability metrics were admittedly mixed. For instance, operating cash flow actually improved from negative $17.9 million to positive $1.2 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from negative $1 million to positive $0.4 million. On the other hand, EBITDA for the company contracted from $7.3 million to $2.9 million.

Management is pushing for continued growth. They said that they expect to open 10 new communities in the second quarter of this year and 18 in the third quarter. Unfortunately, leading indicators are a bit problematic. Net new orders for the company in the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year came in at 296 homes. That's down from the 384 reported the previous year. This, combined with a rise in its cancellation rate, caused a drop in backlog from 262 homes to 201 homes.

In the absence of any real guidance from management, and in light of the overall uncertainties in this space, I think it would be best to value the company based on historical results from the last two fiscal years. In the chart above, you can see how shares are currently priced. Admittedly, the price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple is insanely high. But outside of this, the multiples for the company actually look quite appealing. In the table below, I compared it to five similar enterprises. On a price-to-earnings basis, only one of the five companies was cheaper than it is. And when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, two of the five ended up being cheaper.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
United Homes Group 4.5 72.9 9.6
Landsea Homes Corporation (LSEA) 43.4 7.8 43.5
Legacy Housing Corporation (LEGH) 9.8 18.4 7.3
Hovnanian Enterprises (HOV) 3.5 11.9 4.9
Beazer Homes USA (BZH) 7.2 25.8 13.9
LGI Homes (LGIH) 6.7 117.6 13.7

Takeaway

I am, at heart, a long-term investor. If you asked me what the next year has in store for the homebuilding industry, I would say that things will likely be worse than they were last year. They might even be worse next year than they are this year. But that remains to be seen. What I do know, however, is that the industry will, in the long run, generally do fine. And considering the relative pricing of United Homes Group, and in spite of the weakness that it has experienced, I believe that maintaining a soft ‘buy’ rating is appropriate at this time.

data.title: Coinbase: Powering Ahead Due To Stablecoin
data.description:
As pointed out back in early April, Coinbase Global, Inc. (NASDAQ:COIN) was a buy with the crypto reset near complete when bitcoin slipped to $80K. A couple of months later, the stock is back running towards the highs on positive legislation and higher crypto prices. My investment thesis is still Bullish on the stock due to the massive stablecoin opportunity being unlocked.

Stablecoin Boom Ahead

The U.S. government appears on the verge of establishing rules regarding stablecoin usage. The GENIUS ACT was passed by the Senate and now needs the House of Representatives to pass in order to boost the legitimacy of the crypto industry and provide guardrails on stablecoins by requiring full reserve backing by U.S. dollars and Treasury bills, monthly audits and consumer protections.

Stablecoin offers the opportunity to transact in secure digital assets, bypassing the current financial systems with high costs and slow transaction settlements. The biggest issue has been regulatory uncertainty, now possibly on the path of being resolved.

In connection with Circle Internet Group (CRCL), Coinbase is already in control of the 2nd largest dollar-backed stablecoin, with USDC having $60 billion in assets. Tether is the largest stablecoin at $156 billion, and the company generates billions in annual profits.

Tether and USDC are both 10x the size of the other stablecoins. These 2 coins have huge first mover advantages to take advantage of market growth, while competition is only looking at launching new stablecoins.

The stablecoin market is expected to rise more than tenfold by 2030 to between $3 trillion and $4 trillion, up from only ~$250 billion now. Per analysts at Citizens JMP, stablecoins offer the potential for opening up $100 billion in annual revenue for issuers.

In Q1, Coinbase generated $298 million in stablecoin revenue, up 32% YoY. The company reported $171 million in revenues from off-platform USDC assets of $42 billion.

The crypto firm is already jumping into using USDC for payments and collateral for futures trading. In theory, the stablecoin market increase would drive the current $1+ billion annual revenue run rate to over anywhere from $10 to $15 billion by 2030 alone.

More importantly, fees tied to payments and other usage cases would drive more consistent revenues versus crypto trading transactions reliant on crypto prices. New regulations should bring larger players into the stablecoin market, whether large financial institutions, tech companies and smaller financial services firms like SoFi Technologies (SOFI) and Robinhood Markets (HOOD), after PayPal Holdings (PYPL) had already launched the PYUSD stablecoin.

Big Quarters Ahead

As predicted, Coinbase provided tepid guidance for Q2 when the company reported in early May. The subscription and services revenue was targeted at $680 million or below, after reporting nearly $700 million in Q1.

Naturally, Bitcoin prices have soared back above $100K, leading to the stock rally prior to the positive stablecoin news. Coinbase should actually report a strong quarter compared to targets.

Analysts currently forecast Q2 revenues of only $1.7 billion. Coinbase should maintain revenues closer to the $2 billion range in Q1, unless seasonal volumes limit transactions during the Q2/Q3 period.

Also, Coinbase is acquiring Deribit for ~$2.9 billion, comprised of $700 million in cash and 11 million shares of Coinbase common stock. Considering the company has a market cap of $75 billion with 271 million shares outstanding, the dilution is nearly immaterial, and the company has USD resources of nearly $10 billion to easily pay the cash portion.

The stock faces some resistance between $300 and $340, but all of the positive legislation in stablecoin and the move into crypto derivatives and the EU license to operate a crypto exchange are huge positives. Coinbase has an EV of ~$69 billion with the net cash position of $6 billion, leaving the stock only trading at ~8x sales targets of $8 billion.

The key is that Coinbase has substantial revenue upside in the years ahead after being stuck in the current revenue range due to volatile crypto trading. The big risk is the U.S. House failing to approve the GENIUS Act, leading to lower crypto prices and trading.

The stock quickly becomes very expensive without a revenue boost from higher crypto prices, further boosted by more stablecoin revenues in the years ahead. If the stock doesn't hold the rally today above $300, investors might want to dumb Coinbase on the negative signal.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Coinbase is seeing massive catalysts for the business. Investors hopefully bought at the lows back in April and can use $300 as a key price for buying more shares.

data.title: Emergent BioSolutions: Strategic Turnaround Proving Tricky, But I'm Still Hopeful
data.description:
Investment Overview

I last covered Emergent Biosciences (NYSE:EBS) in a note for Seeking Alpha in November last year. I discussed how the Gaithersburg, Maryland-based biotech, focused on "providing innovative preparedness and response solutions addressing accidental, deliberate, and naturally occurring Public Health Threats (PHTs)", had suffered a major decline in its share price, after reaching highs of $120 per share during the pandemic.

As I wrote in that note:

Emergent completed its initial public offering (IPO) in 2006, joining the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), its shares priced at $12.5. The company's share price surged >$120 during the early pandemic era, when it provided contract manufacturing services for both AstraZeneca (AZN) and Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) COVID vaccines but suffered significant losses in 2021 owing to vaccine production issues, which led to the FDA investigating issues at its Baltimore based manufacturing plant, and finding deficiencies, leading to delays in rolling out JNJ's vaccine. Reports later suggested that the company may have been forced to dispose of materials equivalent to ~400m vaccine doses.

Emergent management was also asked to testify in front of a House Oversight Committee, in relation to how it was able to win multi-million contracts, despite "a long, documented history of inadequately trained staff and quality control issues".

In summary, the company was in something of a crisis. After earning revenues of $1.6bn and $1.77bn in 2020 and 2021, and operating income of $499m, and $386m, in 2022 and 2023, revenues fell to $1.12bn, and $1.05bn, and operating losses were ($109.3m), and ($192m).

In February 2024, Emergent appointed veteran healthcare leader Joseph Papa, formerly CEO at Bausch & Lomb (BLCO), as its new CEO. Papa opted to put in place a "multi-year plan to stabilize, turnaround and ultimately transform the Emergent business", and by November, Emergent had addressed issues such as debt—net debt was reduced by $206 to $551m in 2024—and outstanding litigation with JNJ.

I gave the stock a "Buy" rating, anticipating that the new CEO would ultimately be able to complete a turnaround of the business, although I also suggested 2025 may be a "down year" for the company.

That has proven to be the case—the stock is down 34% year-to-date (at the time of writing), and down >40% since my note. I did predict that Emergent's problems may not be solved overnight, so, nearly 8 months since my last note, this feels like a good time to assess the current state of play, and whether a "Buy" call is still merited.

Emergent – Recent Progress – Slight Uptick After Q1 Earnings

Emergent shared its Q1 earnings and business updates on May 7th, prompting a slight uptick in the share price, from $4.8 to $6.4. Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings had missed analyst's expectations, with revenues of $194.7m in Q4 and a net loss of ($31.3m), and full-year revenues of $1.04bn, adjusted EBITDA of $183.1m, and a net loss of ($191m), compared to ($761m) in the prior year.

In Q1 2025, Emergent achieved revenues of $222m and reaffirmed guidance for 2025 for $750m–$850m of revenues, and adjusted EBITDA of $150–$200m. Revenues, normalized earnings per share (EPS) of $0.71, and GAAP EPS of $1.19 all exceeded analysts expectations in Q1.

Revenues in Q1 were down 26% year-on-year in Q1, but adjusted EBITDA was up >15%, while operating expenses decreased from $100m in Q1 2024, to $68m. Emergent improved its cash position, to $149m, with $36.5m earned from the sale of its Bayview manufacturing plant in Baltimore.

In my last note, I suggested the site might have to be put to another use—one analyst had suggested it as a manufacturing site for popular GLP-1 weight-loss drugs, but Emergent opted to sell it, alongside its Camden CDMO facility. On the Q1 earnings call, management stressed a "deemphasis of our CDMO business as a driver of growth."

Also on the earnings call, CEO Papa told analysts:

We also expect to benefit from strong receivable cash collections in the second quarter. Our net leverage is now 2.8x adjusted EBITDA, which is a remarkable achievement since it was around 5.7x back in the first quarter of 2024.

As we can see in the slide above (from Emergent's earnings presentation), the main revenue driver in Q1 was Emergent's Smallpox medical countermeasures (MCM), with $107m revenues reported, which came from "both the U.S. government and international customers". Anthrax MCM contributed $48m, down $8m year-on-year, while Narcan revenues fell by >60%, to $45m.

Narcan is a nasal spray approved for the emergency treatment of known or suspected opioid overdose and is arguably Emergent's most important product. On its earnings call, management put the fall in sales down to three factors, as follows:

First, the full impact of reduced pricing in the public interest space began to take effect in late Q2'24. Second, volume was impacted by what we believe was a one-time sale of short-dated inventory to the market by a third-party distributor of generic products. And finally, the federal administration transition caused some purchasing delays as states sought to access funding programs.

Management added that it is already seeing "improved trends in unit volumes" in Q2, but the overall market is becoming more competitive. In its 2024 annual report / 10K submission, Emergent notes competitors including Teva Pharma (TEVA), Amneal Pharmaceuticals (AMRX), and Padagis Pharmaceuticals, which all market and sell a Narcan generic, and Amphastar Pharmaceuticals (AMPH), Teleflex (TFX), Harm Reduction Therapeutics, and Opiant Pharma, who have branded products.

CEO Papa remains optimistic, however, stating that:

Three examples demonstrate NARCAN’s leadership value. Number one, we continue to add new public interest customers and regain and retain important state customers, further demonstrating our ability to address competition.

Second, we are currently in discussions with several major large employers, including one major e-commerce giant to supply thousands of boxes of NARCAN Nasal Spray to their locations.

Third, our NARCAN direct distribution platform and strong relationships with leading distributors represent significant growth potential for the product.

In January, Emergent announced it had also licensed Hikma Pharmaceuticals’ KLOXXADO (naloxone HCl) Nasal Spray, an 8 mg naloxone agent, for a six-year period, bolstering its presence in the market. The product was recently approved in Canada, where Emergent also recently signed a 3-year, $65m deal to supply Narcan.

Looking Ahead – Can Emergent's Business & Stock Maintain An Upward Trajectory?

As we can see in the slide below, providing detailed 2025 guidance, Emergent expects to be profitable — at least on an adjusted basis — this year.

The picture remains complex, however. MCM products are expected to earn the lion's share of revenues, while there may be pressure on the company to ensure Narcan / KLOXXADO revenues increase significantly across the next three quarters.

The outlook for Q2 is modest, with revenues and income expected to fall on a sequential basis, but Emergent says that "we anticipate that our full-year revenue will be weighted more to the second half of 2025 than to the first half".

On the earnings call, CEO Papa highlighted its Tembexa m-pox MCM "represents a significant opportunity for potential organic growth", given ongoing mpox studies in Africa, and highlighted an investment agreement with Swiss-based Rocketvax, through which:

Emergent would lead the U.S. manufacturing and commercialization efforts for four of Rocketvax’s pipeline candidates for infectious diseases, cancer, and autoimmune disorders.

The CEO also pointed to increasing international sales — $91m, or ~40% of sales, in Q1, and stressed that the company has "very limited tariff exposure", manufacturing most of its products in the US, while conceding that "we do obtain at this time, one of the devices for our NARCAN comes from Europe".

Still, as mentioned in my last note, MCM markets are not large, and while the CEO stressed "the world is becoming more dangerous", I would not anticipate major near-term growth in this market, while I'd also harbor some doubts about whether Narcan will recapture the large number of revenues lost in Q1.

Meanwhile, Emergent seems slightly unsure about which elements of its strategic turnaround to action next — management seems to be simultaneously promising to "maintain sufficient cash and liquidity to operate the business", "deploy capital in an effective manner to drive growth", and "consider debt repayments to strengthen our balance sheet and share repurchases to create incremental shareholder value".

Net debt stands at $555m, which is weighing heavily on Emergent's market cap valuation, which is $342m at the time of writing. Anyone considering investing in Emergent must be well aware of the risks involved in backing an indebted company. While the company is forecasting profitability in 2025, even a slight miss would put it in danger of defaulting on debt agreements, which could lead to Emergent declaring itself bankrupt.

Personally, I would expect MCM revenues to be stable or growing and don't see the risk of the new US government administration withdrawing contracts or no longer stockpiling Emergent products as too high, and also view the international expansion as promising.

It's difficult to see what BD the company could do in the near future, while there may not be too many more assets the company can sell to ease the debt burden, having sold various manufacturing sites, and its travel health business to Bavarian Nordic in 2023—milestone payments from this source outstanding are $30m, according to my understanding in notes in the Q1 quarterly report.

Considering all of the above, the sensible move is to downgrade my rating on Emergent from a "Buy", to a "Hold". I don't see compelling growth opportunities in play for the company at this time, and feel concerned about the level of competition for Narcan.

Nevertheless, there are some intriguing new opportunities unfolding, internationally and with the Rocketvax agreement, and there are tangible risks to consider, the main one being Emergent's ongoing ability to meet its debt repayments, I still have faith in the CEO's ability to complete this turnaround, and I have always maintained 2025 would be a tough year.

This is a higher-risk investment and for the average investor, until there is more clarity on the performance of key products, and how net debt can be reduced to a more manageable level, it is probably best to be on the sidelines for the time being, in my view.

I am maintaining my "Buy" call, however, as Emergent stock can be extremely volatile to the upside whenever a public health threat emerges, as it typically does once every couple of years, and I do see performance improving in the second half of 2025. I am sticking my neck out and backing Emergent to recover lost Narcan revenues and pay down debt sufficiently to create more favorable long-term market dynamics.

data.title: Zscaler: Do Not Reach For The Skies
data.description:
Zscaler, Inc. (NASDAQ:ZS) was one of the cybersecurity software firms to take some tariff lemons and make beat-and-raise lemonade. The market appears to have warmly welcomed the strong results, as the stock has re-rated sharply higher. The company has sustained aggressive top-line growth rates alongside a net cash balance sheet and soon-to-be GAAP profitability.

At these levels, though, I question whether there is any margin of safety in the event that growth rates disappoint consensus estimates. I reiterate my neutral rating on ZS stock, as investors should look elsewhere for cheaper bargains in the software sector.

ZS Stock Price

I last covered ZS in March, where I explained why I was downgrading the stock due to my expectations for decelerating growth. The stock has soared 45% since then.

I view the recent price action with great suspicion, as I continue to expect top-line growth to decelerate, even as the market seems to expect an acceleration. ZS stock looks pricey here, for anyone counting.

ZS Stock Key Metrics

ZS is an enterprise tech company in the cybersecurity sector selling zero-trust products. In short, one can view ZS as a “modern firewall,” in that instead of just securing a network or providing a VPN, it allows companies to program which applications and users have access to what data. This kind of customization has only grown more and more valuable with the rise of generative AI.

The most recent quarter saw the company generate 23% YoY revenue growth to $678 million, exceeding guidance of between $665 million to $667 million.

Billings jumped 25% YoY to $785 million, exceeding guidance of $762 million. Management had previously warned investors of potential billings' weakness in the first half, with expectations for a ramp-up in the second half. This quarter has shown that this projected ramp-up has been much stronger than expected.

The company did not show meaningful improvements to profitability after the dramatic improvement last year but did outperform guidance with $147 million of non-GAAP operating profit (over guidance of between $140 million to $142 million).

The company saw its GAAP profitability take a step back in the quarter, but I would not worry too much here, as I expect the company to reach sustainable GAAP profitability whenever management decides it is time. If my prediction for decelerating growth comes true, then that time might be sooner than later.

ZS ended the quarter with $3 billion of cash versus $1.1 billion of debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided for the fourth quarter to see 19.2% YoY growth to revenue of between $705 million to $707 million.

On the conference call, management discussed their acquisition of Red Canary, which was previously announced without much detail. Management noted that they would spend $675 million in cash, with the acquisition being “neutral to our FY’26 consensus operating margin.” Management also noted that given that “a big portion of Red Canary's ARR is concentrated” in non-strategic segments, they would retain “approximately half of the $140 million ARR” that has been previously reported. This suggests that the acquisition was done at roughly a 10x ARR multiple.

Is ZS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, ZS was trading at around 18x sales. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain 17% to 20% top-line growth over the next many years, with essentially no deceleration in growth rates.

Management has given long-term targets for up to 22% non-GAAP operating margins.

If we assume a 21% tax rate, that would imply that the stock is trading at 103x long-term earnings. Even after 6 years of 20% growth, the stock would be trading at 34x 2032e earnings.

The Bulls would contend (and I would agree) that management’s long-term model looks too conservative. One could come to this conclusion by looking at the steep profit margins of mature companies like Adobe (ADBE) and Microsoft (MSFT). If we assume that the company can achieve 35% net margins over the long term, then the stock is currently trading at around 51x long-term earnings. Placed against consensus estimates for around 20% forward growth, that represents a 2.6x price-to-earnings growth ratio (PEG ratio). I typically try to buy growth stocks when they trade in the 1x to 2x PEG ratio range. Viewed another way, if we assume the company sustains 20% growth for 6 years (ahead of consensus), then the stock is trading at around 6x 2032e sales or 17x long-term earnings. If we then assume that the stock trades at 10x sales by then, which I note would be a premium multiple as most software names in my coverage trade below 7x sales, then the stock would be priced for around 9% potential annual returns over the next 6 years.

That would likely be enough to pace the broader market, but the problem here is that both the growth estimates and the ending valuation assumption look aggressive. This has not allowed for a margin of safety. I instead assume that the company sees a more normal (but still conservative) pace of deceleration in growth rates of approximately 1% per year ending at a 15% growth rate in 2032, and that the stock trades at around 8x sales (still a big premium to software peers). In this case, the company would generate around $8.5 billion in 2032 revenues, and the stock is trading at around 5.6x 2032e sales. The stock would be priced for roughly 6% annual returns over the next 6 years. I am not positive that this prospective return would outperform the broader market and warrant taking a beta risk.

ZS Stock Conclusion

With Zscaler, Inc. stock trading at very rich valuation multiples, it would need to defy the laws of gravity in terms of both revenue growth rates and valuations in order to generate market-beating returns. It can be tempting to reach for growth (a play on the more familiar term to “reach for yield”), as it can feel like the highest quality growth stocks are all commanding generous valuations, but my experience indicates that this would be a mistake as valuations tend to have a curiously reliable case of biting in a market downturn.

I recommend searching for cheaper names in the software sector, as I have for the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my neutral rating for the stock.

data.title: Toast: With Restaurant Comp Sales Slowing, It's Time To Pause This Investment (Downgrade)
data.description:
Even though the stock market is now in modestly higher territory since the beginning of the year, investors shouldn’t ignore some of the more bearish signals that have emerged out of the Q1 earnings season. One of the most vulnerable areas is anything with a direct reliance on discretionary consumer spending—and restaurants are a perfect example of this, with comparable restaurant sales growth slowing dramatically over the past two quarters.

Toast (NYSE:TOST), in my view, is vulnerable to these trends. Though the company earns subscription revenue from its software solutions, it also relies heavily on payment processing to drive growth. With the stock up over 15% this year, I believe investors are underappreciating the potential risk of growth deceleration here.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Toast in March, when the stock was hovering around $36 per share, having just fallen after a poorly received Q4 earnings print. Amid overall market volatility, however, I’m emphasizing active portfolio management: and Toast, at ~20% higher than my entry point, no longer makes the cut. GPV is at risk as consumers pull back on eating out, and a number of publicly traded restaurant chains have faded as price increases have finally begun to receive pushback in the wake of lower transaction counts. Toast can’t escape the risks its industry faces, and as the stock trades at elevated valuations, I’m dropping my rating here to neutral.

To me, at current share prices, Toast is a mixed bag of positives and negatives. On the bright side for this company:

  • Massive global TAM. Toast estimates its global TAM at $110 billion, indicating that the company’s current ~$5 billion revenue run rate is only less than 5% penetrated into its overall market. This gives Toast plenty of room to expand, especially overseas.
  • A solution for both chain restaurants and high-end fare. Toast is already nationally used by many chain juggernauts; the company recently signed its biggest deal yet with the parent company of Applebee’s. But it’s also encroaching into the territory of smaller independent, but high-ticket restaurants. The company noted earlier this year that a third of James Beard award winners and half of the restaurants in the U.S. Michelin guide are powered by Toast.

At the same time; however, there is also an abundance of risks to consider:

  • Reliance on payment processing revenue exposes Toast to volatile restaurant trends. Less than 20% of Toast’s revenue actually derives from stable subscription revenue. As restaurants report weaker sales amid tighter consumer budgets, Toast will see both its GPV and its revenue growth slow down.
  • Lower margin revenue streams. Again, due to the company’s reliance on payment processing, Toast actually has lower gross margins relative to the rest of the tech industry, at ~25%. In spite of this, the company continues to command valuation multiples as if it were a pure software company.

Let’s dig now into Toast’s valuation. At current share prices near $43, the company has a market cap of $24.55 billion. After we net off the $1.49 billion of cash on Toast’s most recent balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $23.06 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, Toast has guided to $540-560 million on adjusted EBITDA, up 6% from its prior view of $510-530 million (the midpoint of which represents a 9.1% margin on consensus revenue expectations of $6.02 billion for the year). This puts Toast’s valuation at 42x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA.

Toast, in other words, is priced for perfection and hyper-growth: even though the industry it's in is showing severe cracks. I’d take advantage of Toast’s rally as an opportunity to sell and lock in gains.

Q1 download

Let’s now go through Toast’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Toast’s revenue grew 24% y/y to $1.337 billion, actually slightly missing Wall Street’s expectations of $1.343 billion (+25% y/y) by a one-point margin. The company’s growth also decelerated quite sharply versus 29% y/y growth in Q4.

Yes, it’s true that Toast has done a phenomenal job at landing new customers, particularly with multi-location national chains. Q1 saw new deal wins with major national franchises including Dine Brands (DIN), the parent company behind Applebee’s, and Topgolf. The number of locations using the Toast PoS grew 25% y/y to 140k.

Management notes that its sales momentum has been excellent, with average account executive productivity higher y/y and win rates remaining elevated. Per CEO Aman Narang’s remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Our recipe continues to be at the intersection of a vertical platform approach combined with a differentiated in-market go-to-market team. This drives strong win rates where we win a majority of the time across all of our key competitors. We grew locations in essentially all of our markets across our SMB TAM versus last year and our flywheel markets continue to see above-average rep productivity.

Sales AE productivity across new bookings was up in Q1 year-over-year, which is what is giving us confidence in Q2 and the balance of the year. The product and engineering teams continue to leverage both customer and sales feedback to prioritize the road map needed to support durable location growth in our core segment over the long term.”

And yet, there is plenty of cause to be concerned. Toast’s GPV, which is a measure of the total payments processed on the Toast PoS and the biggest driver of the company’s long-term growth trajectory, slowed meaningfully to just 22% growth—well below revenue growth, and 4 points weaker than 26% growth in the previous quarter.

I also continue to keep an eye out on some of the most notable restaurant chain sales to gauge the health of the restaurant industry. Chipotle (CMG), one of the bellwethers in the sector, surprised the markets by slipping to a -0.4% comparable restaurant sales trend, 580bps weaker quarter over quarter.

Dine Brands, which Toast just signed and called its biggest deal ever, fared even worse with a -2.2% decline in domestic same-store systemwide sales for Applebee’s, and -2.6% for IHOP:

And it’s not just older traditional chain concepts that are struggling: Sweetgreen (SG), which was one of the hottest stocks in the restaurant sector in 2024 for its innovations in robotics to automate the salad-making process, is also buckling under macro pressure this year. Here, comp sales dipped -3.1% y/y, decelerating 7 points from 4% growth in Q4.

The point here is that while we applaud Toast’s ability to acquire new locations, new location growth will be offset by lower GPV volumes per location if negative same-store/comp sales trends persist. To that extent, we have to repeat the question: is Toast really worth such a large valuation premium?

For the moment, Toast’s adjusted EBITDA leaped more than 2x y/y to $133 million, or a 9.9% margin that improved 460bps y/y from 5.3% in the year-ago quarter.

The issue, however, is that Toast is already working with relatively thin gross margins—at least relative to the rest of the tech sector. It will rely heavily on volume to keep driving profit growth—and with GPV decelerating and restaurant sector same-restaurant sales growth also softening, we expect Toast’s adjusted EBITDA growth to moderate as well.

Key takeaways

Toast was a great buy when the stock was crashing in March and April, but now I think Toast’s rebound rally has run its course, especially with the restaurant sector outlook darkening. I expect Toast’s valuation multiples to compress as GPV, revenue, and adjusted EBITDA all see more moderate growth throughout the remainder of the year.

Don’t miss your chance to lock in gains here, and move to the sidelines until a better price re-emerges for this stock.

data.title: Innovative Industrial: A Dividend Cut Is Coming, Buy The 13% Yield Anyways
data.description:
Innovative Industrial Properties (NYSE:IIPR) has seen its stock continue to struggle as it has dealt with a rotating cycle of troubled tenants. It is looking increasingly likely that the company will have to cut its dividend at some point, but the market looks overly pessimistic. The company has a strong balance sheet and there is a great margin of safety in the stock price. I also discuss the preferred stock which looks interesting for more conservative investors. I reiterate my strong buy rating for IIPR.

IIPR Stock Price

I last covered IIPR in March where I rated the stock a strong buy on account of the low valuation. The stock has slightly underperformed the broader market since then.

The lack of visible catalysts appears to have kept the dividend yield quite high. Those with some patience might find this to be an attractive buying opportunity.

IIPR Stock Key Metrics

IIPR is known as the largest publicly traded cannabis REIT. It utilizes triple net leases, similar to well known names like Realty Income (O), and meaning that the tenants are responsible for real estate taxes, insurance, and maintenance expenses. With cannabis still being illegal even medicinally across many states, IIPR has significant geographic concentration to legal markets.

IIPR’s top tenants are in general well known multi-state operators (‘MSOs’), but the company’s heavy concentration has come into focus amidst tenant struggles. For example, the top tenant PharmaCann is currently in default.

In return for the higher tenant risk, cannabis leases tend to come at higher cap rates (over 12% versus the typical 6% to 7% industry average) as well as higher annual lease escalators (nearly 3% versus the 1% industry average). IIPR also differs from conventional NNN REITs with its strong balance sheet. Debt to EBITDA stood at under 1x in the quarter, which compares very favorably to the typical 5x to 6x ratios seen at peers.

In the latest quarter, IIPR saw adjusted funds from operations (‘AFFO’) decline 12% YoY as the company continued to work through troubled tenants. The company notably raised $9.7 million from issuing its Series A Preferred stock (IIPR.PR.A) and repurchased $20.1 million of common stock.

On the conference call, management noted that they had issued default notices to the aforementioned PharmaCann, 4Front Ventures (OTCQX:FFNTF), and TILT holdings (OTCQB:TLLTF). FFNTF recently filed for bankruptcy in Canada, while TLLTF curiously resumed paying at least partial rent. Management noted that they had leased a 205,000 sq ft facility in Michigan in April that was formerly occupied by PharmaCann, but with PharmaCann making up over 16% of ABR in the quarter, I expect it to remain a cloud for a while. Management estimated that revenue from the above three tenants included security deposits totaling around $5.8 million or around $0.20 per share. This is important because there are no more security deposits from these tenants that can be applied. Management noted that they are prepared to evict these tenants and take possession. This would not affect their ability to be listed on the New York Stock Exchange (which has banned “plant-touching” companies from listing) because they would utilize a “MSA management service agreement” to help the transition.

Is IIPR Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

Over the years, IIPR has seen sentiment shift from viewing it as a high-growth REIT deserving of a big premium (and a sub-2% dividend yield), to a highly distressed name in secular decline trading at 13% dividend yield.

Yes, the dividend looks to be in great risk as at least 23% of revenues are coming from the three troubled tenants. If we assume that the company is unable to recover any of these properties, then AFFO might decline by 30%, at which point the dividend would no longer be covered.

Yet that appears to be too pessimistic of an outcome for those properties. Anybody who has tried the cannabis product can likely agree that it basically offers all the “fun” of alcohol with far less damage to the body. The fact that this remains a controversial view even in my home state of California, where cannabis use is among the highest in the nation, is arguably a clear sign of the long term growth runway for usage. Whether or not that leads to strong profits for the operators is another story entirely, but my point is that it does not appear reasonable to assume that IIPR would not be able to re-lease struggling properties, or at least reach an appropriate resolution by reducing rents.

We can consider a second scenario in which the company is required to reduce rents on all of its properties by 50%. This would greatly improve the credit profiles of the tenant roster at the obvious cost of IIPR’s own profitability. In the most recent quarter, IIPR generated $55.3 million in AFFO on $71.7 million in revenue, representing a high 77% net profit margin. I again remind that IIPR’s net leases afford it a higher “real” profit margin relative to many REIT peers such as mall or shopping center REITs, with the latter having to invest significant capital in maintenance expenses (something that tenants take care of under net leases). A 50% decline in revenue would lead to a 65% decline in AFFO in the worst case. That would lead to roughly $2.73 AFFO per share annual run-rate. The stock would be trading at around 21x that number. It is not crazy to believe that the stock can sustain that multiple given the aggressive annual lease escalators and strong balance sheet. Yet even if the stock trades down to 15x AFFO, that would reflect around 30% potential downside (and that is before accounting for any dividend payments), which looks arguably modest given the bearish assumptions. I expect the stock to re-rate to a 9% to 10% yield over time, but admittedly it may be a bumpy road to get there.

On a side note, the preferred stock IIPR.PR.A looks quite interesting here at a 9% dividend yield. The preferred stock does not offer as much capital appreciation upside due to the $25 call price, but I view the payment as being rather safe, as it is usually the balance sheet which causes the worst scenario (and the balance sheet is quite strong here).

IIPR Stock Risks

It is possible that the storm never ends. This might occur if the legal adult-use business model simply proves unsustainable over the long term. It is possible that the government does not re-schedule cannabis or even enacts new regulations that hurt the industry. IIPR’s leverage is low but can quickly rise if its rent reductions prove more aggressive than anticipated. Experienced dividend investors know that dividend cuts are seldom, if ever, priced into the stock. IIPR is likely to sustain great volatility moving forward as it is not clear when the uncertainty will end.

IIPR Stock Conclusion

The cannabis sector has seen dark clouds as many operators appear close or in the process of declaring bankruptcy. That has led to the stock of IIPR to trade at elevated dividend yields. A dividend cut looks likely, but the company maintains a strong balance sheet and the valuation looks attractive nonetheless. More conservative investors might want to consider the preferred stock. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

data.title: MercadoLibre: The Best Anti-Dollar Stock
data.description:
Thesis Summary

MercadoLibre, Inc. (NASDAQ:MELI) has sold off over the last month following some significant developments like the CEO transition.

However, nothing has fundamentally changed, and the latest quarterly results still show a company with strong fundamentals.

I remain very bullish on MELI, especially with the dollar entering a bear market. It’s advisable for investors to diversify away from U.S. stocks and the dollar, and MELI is perhaps one of the best ways to do this.

The growth story in Argentina is also a notable bullish catalyst.

The company is still firing on all cylinders, and the current selloff gives us a good spot to add some more shares.

Catching Up With MercadoLibre

A couple of important events have taken place since our last MELI report.

Firstly, MercadoLibre announced a CEO transition, with Marcos Galperin being replaced by Ariel Szarfsztejn.

Commerce President Ariel Szarfsztejn was named as the upcoming CEO and President. Szarfsztejn joined MercadoLibre (NASDAQ:MELI) in 2017. Before his current role, Szarfsztejn held several leadership positions with the Company, including Vice President of Strategy & Corporate Development in 2017, Vice President of Mercado Envios from 2018 to 2020, Senior Vice President and Head of Mercado Envios from 2020 to 2021 and Executive Vice President of Commerce from 2022 to 2023.

Source: SA Press Release.

While the stock has sold off since, this is a very orderly transition, with a CEO ready to retire being replaced by a competent employee who has been with the company for almost a decade and risen through the ranks.

The other important recent update is MELI lowering its free shipping limit, a move that has had a mixed response from investors.

While this will certainly make MELI more competitive and likely help increase GMV, it will also take a toll on the company’s margins and free cash flow, which already took a hit last quarter.

Q1 2025

MELI maintained a strong growth trajectory in Q1. Marketplace GMV grew by 40%.

And Fintech services more than doubled YoY and showed a very encouraging quarterly growth.

The only “issue” is that profitability is not moving in the right direction.

Operating margin fell to 12.9%, and income margin was 8.3%.

On the commerce side, there have been higher costs associated with logistics and promotions, and on the fintech side, we have seen a lower Net Interest Margin as the company expands and also moves into credit cards.

And cash flows were negative due to a $256 million capex investment in logistics.

Great Growth In Argentina

Although there were some concerns on MELIs profitability front, these are still regular growing pains, and I still defend the thesis that it’s much more important to focus on growth right now than earnings.

Improving GMV, and gaining traction in fintech and other key markets in Latin America should be priority number one.

I was particularly impressed by the growth we saw in Argentina.

Commerce and fintech revenues more than doubled YoY here. Argentina is a country undergoing a very significant economic turnaround, and it’s good to see MELI outperform here.

So we think we are very optimistic about Argentina, not only because of macro, but more important than that, because of all -- the brand that we have in Argentina, both from MercadoLibre and Mercado Pago, the user experience that we have in Argentina are performing extremely well, as Ariel mentioned, both in e-commerce as well as fintech and we will continue to deploy and improve the user experience in Argentina, just like we are doing in the other countries.

Source: Earnings Call.

This is a country that is quickly becoming much wealthier, with a population of over 45 million.

The Anti-Dollar Trade

With the dollar experiencing one of its worst starts of the year in the last century, I think it’s also a good idea to own MELI right now.

MELI benefits from a weaker dollar, since its earnings are nondollar denominated, generally, so this is actually a big currency tailwind.

Furthermore, the company offers geographical diversification away from the US, which is also key in today’s geopolitical environment.

Valuation

I won’t deny that a 50 P/E seems rich, but with a PEG still below 1, there’s room for upside when it comes to MELI.

Valuations are frothy all around right now. I’m not sure if it will last, but even if it doesn’t, I want exposure to a quality company like MELI that offers diversification.

Technical Analysis

MELI does have a tendency to retest its 50-day EMA, something it has done quite a few times in this bull market.

It's possible that we will get a chance to buy up MELI close to the $2000 area, also at an important psychological level.

Risks

MELI is expanding rapidly, and that comes with some risks.

The fintech side, for example, could suffer from worsening margins and worse quality, at least in the short term.

On the commerce side, fast growth will be accompanied by higher costs, especially with the new shipping terms.

Obviously, we want MELI to grow, but persistent negative cash flow would begin to hurt the financial health of the company too, which should be avoided.

Final Thoughts

All in all, MercadoLibre, Inc. continues to be a good buy. I am particularly optimistic about the strong Argentina growth and love the fact that this offers exposure to economies that are not U.S. dollar-denominated.

data.title: GMS Is The Right Fit For QXO's Long-Term Goal
data.description:
The QXO and GMS Investment Thesis

QXO, Inc. (NYSE:QXO) is a promising company that aims to acquire a large share of the construction industry market through aggressive M&A. And the latest acquisition rumors regarding GMS Inc. (NYSE:GMS) have brought QXO back into the spotlight.

I believe this planned acquisition will increase value, and I am convinced by QXO's strategy. Therefore, despite the fact that they might appear expensive to traditional value investors, I consider the stock to be promising.

The strategy of QXO and how I became aware of it

Last October, I wrote an article about Beacon Roofing Supply (BECN) because I found their capital allocation strategy very interesting. Shortly thereafter, they were acquired by QXC, which I covered in an article from March 2025, shortly before the deal was finalized. This led me to take a closer look at QXO.

Because I place great importance on capital allocation and management expertise, I was particularly impressed by Brad Jacobs's résumé. He has achieved significant success with XPO (XPO), United Rentals (URI), and United Waste Systems (WM), as well as their spinoffs, and has generated a lot of shareholder value.

In my opinion, the most important factors for the success were the exceptional M&A activities and the development of highly talented employees who perform exceptionally well as part of a team. In other words, I clearly see QXO as a bet on Brad Jacobs and the culture of his company providing a competitive advantage.

To build the right team, Ashwin Rao was brought in, who previously worked as Vice President of AI at Target (TGT). He has the necessary expertise to establish AI-driven demand forecasting and pricing as a competitive advantage. Furthermore, Ihsan Essaid was hired as CFO who previously was the Global Head of M&A at Barclays. So another person with high expertise in his field.

QXO's plan is now to consolidate the construction industry and optimize business processes with the help of AI. The first step was the acquisition of Beacon Roofing Supply, which now gives QXO access to more than 500 locations in the US and Canada. The goal, however, is to reach an annual revenue of $50 billion within a decade. So to achieve this goal, further acquisitions are necessary, as Beacon Roofing Supply only generated about $10 billion annually.

What impact did the Beacon Roofing Supply deal have on liquidity?

To finance the acquisition, QXO completed a $2.25 billion offering of 6.75% Senior Secured Notes due 2032. Jacobs acknowledged the sizable debt but was quick to note that deleveraging is a priority. “We’re targeting a 4x net leverage ratio by 2026. That’s very achievable given Beacon’s cash flow profile and our operating improvements,” he said.

Assuming the net debt of 4x is reached by 2026, I would consider the liquidity/debt position to be strong. This is especially true given my belief that the roofing materials industry has a bright future.

What are the plans for the future, and what is GMS's role in this context?

QXO has made an offer to purchase all of GMS's outstanding shares at $95.20 each, which would equate to approximately $5 billion. This tender offer is available until June 24. After that, the offer will be made directly to the GMS shareholders.

With over 300 locations and approximately 100 tool sales, rental, and service centers currently owned by GMS, QXO could establish a strong presence in the construction products industry, potentially leading to future dominance. GMS has also frequently expanded its product range and geographical reach through roll-ups, which I believe strengthens its alignment with QXO.

However, since Brad Jacobs's playbook is known and the established players on the market would naturally like to maintain their competitive position, there are now additional reports that Home Depot (HD) could also be interested in GMS. I think this is probably an attempt to prevent QXO from growing too quickly and becoming dominant in the market. Of course, GMS would also fit with Home Depot. However, I believe it is more likely the latter. Plus it could also be an attempt to drive up the price that QXO must pay.

Now that other major players are involved, the deals may not be as lucrative as before. But the same was thought in the other industries Brad Jacobs has successfully entered. But as is often the case, a first-class management team is the most important competitive advantage.

Valuation

Since investing always involves looking into the future, and since QXO's goal is to reach revenues of $50 billion, we can use peer comparisons to determine the price at which QXO would trade. Since competitors usually have a PS multiple of 1–2x, this would likely make QXO worth between $50 and $100 billion. This would be a huge increase from the current $13 billion.

However, this also means that QXO is currently trading at a premium compared to Builder FirstSource (BLDR), with a PS ratio likely in the range of ~1.3x. But this also demonstrates the market's confidence in Brad Jacobs's abilities. Because if the market now believes that a multiple in the range of Lowe's is appropriate, this is a sign of appreciation.

Conclusion

Should the QXO/GMS deal come to fruition, I believe it would be highly valuable for QXO, as it aligns with the strategy outlined by the management team. Additionally, more acquisitions will probably follow in the coming years, all of which aim to consolidate the market.

I have a lot of respect for Brad Jacobs' abilities, so I will build a small entry position over the next few days to continue observing the situation. But I am also aware that the shares are not currently cheap and that this is a bet on the management team and its capital allocation.
